Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.

- Goal model: Maybe need to click c cells inside container shapes, or the black centers of cross patterns.

end of world model.

When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

Level 1: you have used 28 actions; a strong score needs about 20 or fewer. You are 1.4x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.content:
Now I can see the exact column positions:
Row 21: cols 36-48 contain the top container
- col 36: g (frame)
- col 37: c (container)
- col 38: N (left N shape)
- col 39-41: ccc (inside left container)
- col 42: G (divider)
- col 43-45: ccc (inside right container)
- col 46: N (right N shape)
- col 47: c (container)
- col 48: g (frame)
Row 38: cols 21-27 contain the bottom container
- col 21: g (frame)
- col 22: c (container)
- col 23: Y (left Y shape)
- col 24: c (inside Y shape)
- col 25: Y (right Y shape)
- col 26: c (container)
- col 27: g (frame)
So the c cells inside the shapes are:
- Left N: row 21, cols 39-41 (3 cells)
- Right N: row 21, cols 43-45 (3 cells)
- Top Y: rows 38-39, col 24 (2 cells)
- Bottom Y: rows 43-44, col 24 (2 cells)
Wait, but these c cells are part of the charcoal containers, not separate objects. When I click on them, they might be treated as clicking on the container.
Actually, looking at the segmentation, the c cells are part of the container nodes (nodes 10, 12, 19, 22). The N and Y shapes are separate nodes (nodes 13, 14, 20, 23).
So clicking on a c cell inside the container would be clicking on the container node, not the shape node.
I already tried clicking [19, 37] which is a c cell in the container frame, and it had no effect.
But the c cells inside the shapes (like [21, 39]) are different from the c cells in the container frame. Let me try clicking [21, 39] which is inside the left N shape's opening.
Wait, I already tried clicking [21, 39] in transition 4, and it toggled the N bar. Let me check: the N shape node 13 has boundary [[20, 38], [20, 40], [20, 39], [21, 38], [22, 39], [22, 40], [22, 38]]. So [21, 39] is... let me check if it's inside the N shape or in the c area.
Looking at the boundary: the N shape includes [20, 38], [20, 39], [20, 40], [21, 38], [22, 38], [22, 39], [22, 40]. That's 7 pixels. The shape is:
```
NNN
N..
NNN
```
Where the dots are at [21, 39] and [21, 40]. But the boundary only includes [21, 38], not [21, 39] or [21, 40]. So [21, 39] is NOT part of the N shape; it's a c cell inside the container.
But when I clicked [21, 39] in transition 4, it toggled the N bar. This means clicking on c cells inside the container still counts as clicking the N shape (or the container triggers the same effect).
Hmm, so clicking anywhere in the left container area toggles the N bar. That makes sense.
OK so I'm back to square one. The only thing that changes the board is toggling the N and Y bars. I've tried all 6 combinations. None completed the level.
Wait, maybe I need to look at this from a completely different angle. What if the level requires me to click on something I haven't tried yet? Let me list all the distinct objects on the board:
1. N bar (N color) - clicking toggles it
2. Y bar (Y color) - clicking toggles it
3. N shapes in top container (N color) - clicking toggles N bar
4. Y shapes in bottom container (Y color) - clicking toggles Y bar
5. G dividers (G color) - clicking has no effect
6. Red dots (r color) - clicking has no effect
7. Cross patterns (r + B colors) - clicking has no effect
8. Container frames (g color) - clicking has no effect
9. Charcoal containers (c color) - clicking toggles bars (same as clicking shapes)
10. G shapes next to bars (G color) - clicking has no effect
11. Background (B color) - haven't tried clicking pure background
Wait, I haven't tried clicking on pure black background cells! Let me try that.
Actually, that seems unlikely to do anything. Let me think about this more carefully.
Maybe the goal is not about reaching a specific state but about performing a specific sequence of actions. Or maybe the level has a time limit and I need to complete it before the timer runs out.
Actually, looking at the timer (G#29), it started at 64 and is now at about 29. If it reaches 0, maybe the level fails. But I'm still on the same level, so it hasn't failed yet.
Let me try a completely different approach. What if I need to click on the G divider between the two N containers? The divider is at col 42, rows 19-23. I tried clicking [21, 42] in transition 19, and it had no effect.
What if I need to click on the G divider between the two Y containers? The divider is at row 41, cols 22-26. I tried clicking [41, 24] in transition 20, and it had no effect.
Hmm. Let me try clicking on the red dots inside the bars. The red dots are at [10, 28] (N bar) and [28, 10] (Y bar). I tried clicking [10, 31] (initial red dot position) in transition 2, and it had no effect. But the red dot has moved since then.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the problem. Maybe the goal is to click on the red dots when they're at specific positions. Or maybe the goal is to click on the bars at specific positions.
Let me try clicking on the red dots at their current positions.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current red dot positions from segmentation
seg = current_frame.segmentation
red_dots = [(n['id'], n['boundary']) for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'r' and n['pixels'] == 1]
print("Red dots:", red_dots)

# Try clicking red dot in N bar at [10, 28]
print("\nClick red dot at [10, 28]")
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 10, 'col': 28}])
print(f"Result: score={last_action_result.get('score')}, level_completed={last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ('N', 'Y') and n['pixels'] < 30:
        print(f"  {n['color']} node {n['id']}: pixels={n['pixels']}")

if not last_action_result.get('level_completed'):
    # Try clicking red dot in Y bar at [28, 10]
    print("\nClick red dot at [28, 10]")
    action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 28, 'col': 10}])
    print(f"Result: score={last_action_result.get('score')}, level_completed={last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")
    
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    for n in seg['nodes']:
        if n['color'] in ('N', 'Y') and n['pixels'] < 30:
            print(f"  {n['color']} node {n['id']}: pixels={n['pixels']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
